text.title: Five Viral Loop Archetypes
text.summary: Word of Mouth, Inherent, Incentivized, Content, and Outbreak loops with K ranges, characteristics, and real examples.

section.title: Loop Mechanics
section.p: All viral loops share the same 5-step cycle: user joins → encounters trigger → sends invitation → friend converts → new user repeats the cycle. Loop type determines where the trigger occurs and how strong it is.

rule.statement: Use WOM when the product is genuinely exceptional and natural share moments exist at peak satisfaction; do not engineer WOM for mediocre products.
rule.rationale: WOM has low i (not everyone tells friends) but high c (personal recommendation converts strongly). Forcing WOM prompts on a product without real delight backfires — users feel pressured and NPS drops.

list.item: Best for: productivity tools, exceptional consumer products, strong brand

rule.statement: Use inherent virality only when the product functionally requires others to participate; do not force collaboration features onto solo-use products.
rule.rationale: Inherent virality has the highest K ceiling because the invite is required, not optional. Forced inherent virality (fake "invite to see content") gets labeled spam and damages NPS.

list.item: Examples: Slack, Zoom, Figma, Google Docs
Best for: collaboration, team, and multi-player products

rule.statement: Use incentivized virality when LTV > 3x CAC and the reward can be sized at 20-50% of CAC without destroying margins; implement double-sided incentives.
rule.rationale: Incentivized virality boosts i but reduces authenticity, lowering c. The net K is viable when the reward economics work. Single-sided referrals feel exploitative and perform 20-30% worse.

list.item: Examples: Dropbox (free storage), Uber ($10 credit), PayPal ($10 cash)
Best for: high-LTV SaaS, fintech, e-commerce with margin room

rule.statement: Use content virality when users naturally produce shareable artifacts (scores, results, badges, created content) that embed product branding without feeling like an ad.
rule.rationale: Content virality has variable i (depends on content quality and shareability) and low c (many views, few signups). The share artifact must have novelty and social value — most imitations fail.

list.item: Best for: content creation platforms, social apps, entertainment products

rule.statement: Do not implement outbreak (automated contact import) virality; modern email filters and OS protections block it, and it damages brand reputation.
rule.rationale: Outbreak virality automates invites to full contact lists. It was viable in 1996 (Hotmail email signature). Today it triggers spam filters, breaches CAN-SPAM, and is rejected by app stores.
